content:
The idea of penalizing large parameters to stabilize learning has roots in **Tikhonov regularization** (1940s–1960s) for ill-posed inverse problems—adding a small term to make unstable solutions well-behaved.

**Ridge regression** (Hoerl & Kennard, 1970) brought L2 penalized least squares to statistics for multicollinear predictors. **Lasso** (Tibshirani, 1996) introduced L1 penalties for simultaneous shrinkage and variable selection.

As neural networks resurged, **weight decay** (L2 on connection weights) became the default regularizer alongside cross-entropy or MSE data losses.

Today L1/L2 regularization is the standard vocabulary for overfitting mitigation across linear models, generalized linear models, and deep learning—unifying shrinkage and sparsity under the single penalized-loss framework **L_total = L_data + λ Ω(θ)**.

timeline.label: Tikhonov regularization (1943, 1960s)
timeline.description: L2-style penalties stabilize ill-posed linear inverse problems—the mathematical ancestor of Ridge shrinkage.

timeline.label: Ridge regression (1970)
timeline.description: Hoerl and Kennard apply L2 penalized least squares to multicollinearity in applied statistics.

timeline.label: Lasso (1996)
timeline.description: Tibshirani introduces L1 penalties, linking sparsity to overfitting control and feature selection.

timeline.label: Weight decay in neural nets (1990s–present)
timeline.description: L2 on network weights becomes standard practice alongside backprop-trained models.
